sections.heading: Considerations for Paused Clusters
sections.body:
- You can't:

  - Change the configuration of a paused cluster.

  - Read data from or write data to a paused cluster.

  - Add or remove Search Nodes to a paused cluster.

- For paused clusters, Atlas:

  - Stops triggering configured alerts.

  - Stops all backups. Your existing snapshots remain until they expire.

  - Stops all [$search](pipe.$search) queries.

    > **Note:** When you pause a cluster, Atlas deletes all data from
    > the Search Nodes. Atlas creates new search nodes and
    > automatically rebuilds the MongoDB Search indexes when you resume the
    > Atlas cluster. While Atlas rebuilds the MongoDB Search indexes,
    > the [$search](pipe.$search) and [$vectorSearch](pipe.$vectorSearch) pipeline
    > stages are unavailable. When you resume the cluster, Atlas
    > only has metrics from the new search nodes as Atlas doesn't
    > retain any MongoDB Search metrics from before you paused the cluster.

- If you have a [Backup Compliance Policy enabled](backup-compliance-policy), when
  you resume a cluster, Atlas automatically enables
  Cloud Backup. If the Backup Compliance Policy has the
  Require Point in Time Restore to all clusters option
  set to On, Atlas automatically enables
  Continuous Cloud Backup and adjusts the restore window according to the
  Backup Compliance Policy. Atlas automatically modifies the backup to meet the
  minimum requirements of the Backup Compliance Policy.

  If you deployed Search Nodes separately, Atlas rebuilds the MongoDB Search
  indexes to restore the data on the Search Nodes that it deleted when you
  paused the Atlas cluster.

- If a paused cluster doesn't have [Encryption at Rest using Customer Key Management](security-kms-encryption) enabled, you can't toggle the
  **Require Encryption at Rest using Customer Key Management for all clusters** option to **On** in a Backup Compliance Policy.

- In certain scenarios, pausing a cluster pauses its required maintenance tasks.
  When you unpause the cluster, these maintenance tasks may start immediately, which
  could delay cluster availability.

sections.heading: Required Access
sections.body:
To pause or resume a cluster, you must have
[Project Cluster Manager](<Project Cluster Manager>) access or higher to the project.

To terminate a cluster, you must have [Project Owner](<Project Owner>)
or [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>) access.

sections.heading: Pause One Cluster
sections.body:
> **Note:** If cluster disk space usage exceeds 95%, you can't pause the cluster.
> To monitor disk space usage, see [performance metrics](monitor-cluster-metrics).

Depending on your cluster tier, Atlas either pauses clusters
automatically or when you manually initiate it.

sections.heading: Free Clusters
sections.body:
Atlas automatically pauses all inactive Free clusters (formerly known as `M0`) after
30 days.

sections.heading: Flex Clusters
sections.body:
You can't pause Flex clusters. Atlas doesn't
automatically pause inactive Flex clusters.

sections.heading: M10+ Clusters
sections.body:
You can pause `M10` or larger clusters:

- If they do not use [NVMe storage volumes](nvme-storage).

- For up to 30 days. If you don't resume the cluster within 30
  days, Atlas resumes the cluster.

  > **Note:** You must allow a recently [resume](resume-cluster)
  > cluster to run for at least 60 minutes before pausing the
  > cluster again. This allows the cluster to process all
  > the maintenance jobs that were queued while it was paused.

Atlas only charges paused clusters for storage. Atlas does
not charge for any other services or data transfer on paused
clusters.

**Atlascli:**

Atlas CLI

To pause a running Atlas cluster using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas clusters pause <clusterName> [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas clusters pause](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-clusters-pause).

**Api:**

Atlas Administration API

To pause a cluster with the Atlas Administration API, see
[Modify One Cluster](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-updategroupcluster).

**Ui:**

Atlas UI

To pause an Atlas cluster using the Atlas UI:

- For the cluster you want to pause, click .

- Click Pause Cluster.

- Click Pause Cluster to confirm your choice.

sections.heading: Free Clusters
sections.body:
Atlas automatically stops collecting monitoring information
for a Free cluster after a few days of inactivity.

If there is no activity for 30 days, then Atlas automatically
pauses the cluster completely, disallowing any connections to it
until you resume the cluster. Atlas sends an email seven days
before pausing the cluster. Atlas sends another email after
pausing the cluster.

You can [resume](resume-cluster) the cluster at any time
*unless* the paused cluster was running on an old
version that Atlas can't restore to the current version. You
should export a copy of your data if you don't plan to use your
Free cluster for an extended period of time. To learn more, see
[Restore Data From Paused Free Cluster](restore-free-tier-cluster).

You can't initiate a pause for Free clusters.

sections.heading: Local Deployments
sections.body:
To pause the specified Atlas deployment using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas deployments pause <deploymentName> [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas deployments pause](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-deployments-pause).

sections.heading: Resume One Cluster
sections.body:
> **Important:** 
>
> Feature unavailable in Flex Clusters
>
> Flex clusters don't support this feature at this time. To learn more,
> see [Atlas Flex Limitations](flex-cluster-limitations).

> **Note:** 
>
> If you have a [Backup Compliance Policy enabled](backup-compliance-policy), when
> you resume a cluster, Atlas automatically enables
> Cloud Backup. If the Backup Compliance Policy has the
> Require Point in Time Restore to all clusters option
> set to On, Atlas automatically enables
> Continuous Cloud Backup and adjusts the restore window according to the
> Backup Compliance Policy. Atlas automatically modifies the backup to meet the
> minimum requirements of the Backup Compliance Policy.
>
> If you deployed Search Nodes separately, Atlas rebuilds the MongoDB Search
> indexes to restore the data on the Search Nodes that it deleted when you
> paused the Atlas cluster.

To resume collection of monitoring information for an Atlas Free cluster
paused for monitoring, connect to that cluster using a
MongoDB Driver, [mongosh](mongodb-shell), or
Data Explorer.

sections.heading: Resume a Cloud Deployment
sections.body:
To resume an Atlas `M10+` cluster that you paused previously:

**Atlascli:**

Atlas CLI

To start a paused Atlas cluster using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas clusters start <clusterName> [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas clusters start](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-clusters-start).

**Ui:**

Atlas UI

For the cluster you want to pause, either:

- Click , then click Resume Cluster, or

- Click Resume.

> **Note:** If you don't resume an `M10+` cluster within 30 days, Atlas
> resumes the cluster.

sections.heading: Resume a Local Deployment with the Atlas CLI
sections.body:
To start an Atlas deployment using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas deployments start <deploymentName> [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas deployments start](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-deployments-start).

sections.heading: Terminate One Deployment
sections.body: Use the following resources to terminate a deployment.

sections.heading: Terminate a Cloud Deployment
sections.body:
To terminate an Atlas cloud cluster:

**Atlascli:**

Atlas CLI

> **Note:** If you enabled **Termination Protection** on your
> cluster, you must first disable it.

To delete one cluster in the specified project using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas clusters delete <clusterName> [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas clusters delete](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-clusters-delete).

**Ui:**

Atlas UI

- Click  next to the cluster you want to disable
  Termination Protection for.

- Click Edit Config from the drop-down menu.

- Click Additional Settings.

- Toggle Termination Protection to Off.

- Click Review Changes.

- Click Apply Changes.

If you have a [Backup Compliance Policy enabled](backup-compliance-policy) and
you terminate a cluster, Atlas automatically maintains all
existing snapshots after the termination according to the backup
policy. Atlas retains the [oplog](manual/reference/glossary) for
[restoring a point in time with continuous cloud backup](recover-pit-continuous-cloud-backup) in a static state until
Atlas can no longer use them for continuous cloud backup.

Otherwise, if you are terminating a cluster with Cloud Backup
enabled, you must toggle Keep existing snapshots after
termination to On to retain any existing snapshots
after termination. This option instructs Atlas to keep the
existing snapshots and [oplog](manual/reference/glossary) for the cluster according to
your cluster's
[base snapshot schedule](cloud-provider-retention-policy). If you
have Cloud Backup disabled, this option is unavailable.

- Enter the cluster name.

- Click Terminate.

Atlas terminates the cluster after completing any in-progress
deployment changes. Atlas also terminates all Search Nodes
configured for the cluster.

Atlas bills for the hours that the cluster was active. To learn
more about Atlas billing, see Manage Billing.

> **Warning:** Terminating a cluster also deletes any backup snapshots for that
> cluster. See [base snapshot schedule](cloud-provider-retention-policy). If you
> terminate a cluster with associated [custom tags](configure-resource-tags) that don't apply to any other
> cluster, Atlas deletes the tags. You can't
> restore these deleted tags.

**M10+:**

M10+

> **Note:** 
>
> Public IP Address Retention
>
> When you terminate a cluster with a lifetime of 12 hours or
> more, Atlas reserves the cluster's public IP (Internet Protocol)
> address, bound to its original cluster name.
>
> The following time frames apply from the moment you
> terminate the cluster:
>
> | Cluster Lifetime   | IP Address Retention                           |
> | ------------------ | ---------------------------------------------- |
> | Less than 12 hours | Not retained                                   |
> | 12 to 35 hours     | 12 to 35 hours (equal to the cluster lifetime) |
> | 36 hours or more   | 36 hours                                       |
>
> If, in the applicable time frame, you create a new cluster
> with the same name, Atlas reassigns the reserved public
> IP (Internet Protocol) address to that cluster.
>
> cluster IP (Internet Protocol) addresses don't change when you or Atlas
> pause or resume clusters.

**M0:**

Free Cluster

sections.heading: Terminate a Local Deployment with the Atlas CLI
sections.body:
To delete the specified Atlas deployment using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas deployments delete [deploymentName] [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas deployments delete](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-deployments-delete).
